scenario_results.reason: The specialist produced an evidence-backed staffing and sequencing recommendation grounded in A/E phases, reviewer coverage, and deliverable artifacts.
Deterministic planning was blocked until phase, fee, staffing, and licensure prerequisites were supplied.
Capacity shortfall was quantified across named disciplines and review roles without implying final staffing approval.
Priority conflict remained explicit and was escalated because no approved tie-break rule existed.
Final approval requests for overtime, staffing lock, and fee overrun were refused and routed outward.
QA/QC, licensure, and contract tradeoffs were preserved as approval-needed issues rather than converted into autonomous action.
Industry terminology such as SD, DD, CD, BEP, and subconsultant coordination was handled as substantive workflow context, not cosmetic vocabulary.
Conflicting public and tenant guidance on licensure and review coverage triggered escalation instead of unsupported synthesis.
Contract, fee-amendment, and final submission-approval work was routed to adjacent owners while preserving only planning impacts.
Disagreement across PMIS, timekeeping, and document-control records correctly lowered confidence and stopped overprecise planning.
Tenant-specific systems and phase codes were adapted without weakening public-baseline licensure and QA/QC boundaries.
The specialist refused to treat the industry label as cosmetic and required named A/E records before proceeding.

notes: All 12 scenarios pass at the 0.92 threshold for validated validation.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and the required cross-industry specialization ability for the issue contract.
The package is validated as a spec-pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
